title: Shingles (Herpes Zoster)
oneLiner: Reactivation of varicella-zoster virus causing a painful, unilateral vesicular rash in a single dermatome; common in elderly/immunocompromised, risk of postherpetic neuralgia.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Disseminated zoster** (visceral / pneumonitis / encephalitis) — key finding: **widespread vesicles beyond original dermatome** + systemic illness (fever, hypoxia, confusion)
- **Herpes zoster ophthalmicus** with acute retinal necrosis / keratitis — key finding: **vesicles on nose tip (Hutchinson's sign)** + eye pain, reduced vision, photophobia
- **Bacterial superinfection** leading to sepsis — key finding: **spreading cellulitic erythema, purulent exudate, fever** in an otherwise mild rash
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Prodrome: unilateral dermatomal pain / burning / paraesthesia (often precedes rash by 2-3 days)
  - Rash: crops of vesicles on erythematous base, **unilateral, in a single dermatome** (thoracic > trigeminal > lumbar)
  - Pain: constant / lancinating, may be severe
  - Systemic: malaise, low-grade fever, headache (mild unless disseminated)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Grouped vesicles (≤5 mm) that crust over 7-10 days, often leaving scarring / pigment change
  - Allodynia / hyperalgesia over the affected dermatome
  - Lymphadenopathy in draining nodes (occasional)
  - Signs of complications: eye redness, corneal ulcer, hearing loss (Ramsay Hunt), limb weakness (motor involvement)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - **Herpes simplex (HSV)** — recurrent, smaller clusters (often perioral / genital), **no dermatomal single-dermatome pattern**, typically milder pain before rash
  - **Contact dermatitis** — **itching > pain**, vesicular but not dermatomal, history of new topical agent / irritant
  - **Impetigo** — honey-crusted sores, **no dermatomal distribution**, often on face/limbs, more common in children
  - **Cellulitis** — diffuse erythema, swelling, warmth, **no vesicles**, no dermatomal border
  - **Varicella (chickenpox)** — widespread vesicles in different stages, **generalised pruritus**, systemic illness in children/adults
- **Splitter:** one feature that sharply differentiates top two (zoster vs HSV): **single dermatome involvement with prodromal pain** — zoster; recurrent episodes at same site + smaller clusters — HSV. If unsure, swab for viral PCR.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** Clinical diagnosis sufficient in typical cases. Slit-lamp exam if any suspicion of eye involvement (trigeminal branch V1 → urgent ophthalmology).
- **Bloods:** Not needed for routine diagnosis. Consider FBC, CRP, U&E, LFT if immunocompromised or suspected disseminated disease.
- **Imaging:** None routinely. CT/MRI brain if suspected encephalitis / CNS involvement (uncommon).
- **Special tests:**
  - **Vesicle swab for varicella-zoster virus (VZV) PCR** — gold standard if atypical, immunocompromised, or treatment failure. (Use dry swab, unroof vesicle, swab base. Send to virology.)
  - **Tzanck smear** (multinucleated giant cells) — rapid but less specific; PCR preferred.
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:** No formal scoring; diagnosis clinical — dermatomal vesicular rash with prodromal pain.
- **Never-miss test:** **Slit-lamp examination** when rash is in V1 distribution (forehead, nose tip) — Hutchinson’s sign predicts eye involvement. Omission may lead to blindness.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If ophthalmic zoster suspected → **urgent ophthalmology referral** (within 1-2 hours)
  - Start analgesia (paracetamol + NSAID + neuropathic agent if pain severe)
  - Initiate antivirals within 72 hours of rash onset (or if immunocompromised, regardless of timing)
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Only admit if: widespread disease, immunocompromised, ophthalmic with need for IV therapy, severe pain requiring IV analgesia, or suspected dissemination/encephalitis
  - **IV aciclovir** for disseminated zoster, ophthalmic with keratitis/retinal involvement, immunocompromised patients (e.g. transplant, HIV, chemotherapy)
  - Daily review of rash progression, eye symptoms, pain scores
  - Pain management: adjuvant gabapentin/pregabalin if neuropathic component
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Complete 7 days of oral antiviral (see Drugs & Doses)
  - Analgesia stepped as needed: paracetamol → NSAID → topical lidocaine → gabapentin/pregabalin (for postherpetic neuralgia)
  - Advise: keep rash clean and dry, avoid scratching, no vigorous exercise until crusted
  - **Vaccination:** Offer VZV vaccine (Shingrix or Zostavax) for prevention of recurrences in at-risk patients ≥50 years (per UK NHS schedule)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: IV 0.9% sodium chloride 1 L/8h if receiving IV aciclovir (to reduce risk of crystalluria)
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD if >18 and mobilising poorly (or according to local policy)
  - Diet: normal, ensure adequate hydration
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly if stable, 1-2 hourly if unstable or on IV therapy
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Oral aciclovir** 800 mg 5 times daily for 7 days (NICE/BNF) — start within 72 hours of rash
  - **IV aciclovir** 10–15 mg/kg every 8 hours (usually 500 mg–1 g Q8H) — for disseminated/immunocompromised/ophthalmic (check local policy for exact dose)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Valaciclovir** 1 g TDS for 7 days (oral, better bioavailability)
  - **Famciclovir** 500 mg TDS for 7 days (oral)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Aciclovir: caution in renal impairment (dose adjustment required)
  - Valaciclovir: same renal caution
  - Interaction: probenecid increases aciclovir levels; avoid concurrent nephrotoxins (e.g., gentamicin, NSAIDs in high doses)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - **Aciclovir (oral):** CrCl 10–25 mL/min → 800 mg TDS; CrCl <10 → 800 mg BID (BNF)
  - **IV aciclovir:** CrCl 25–50: 10–15 mg/kg Q12H; CrCl 10–25: 10–15 mg/kg Q24H; CrCl <10: 5–7.5 mg/kg Q24H (check local policy)
  - Valaciclovir/famciclovir also require dose reduction in moderate–severe renal impairment (refer to BNF)
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **IV aciclovir — give too fast or without adequate hydration → acute renal failure due to crystalluria.** Ensure infusion over ≥1 hour, pre- and post-hydration, and monitor U&E daily. Also: **prescribing for ophthalmic zoster without urgent ophthalmology review** = delayed sight loss.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Postherpetic neuralgia (most common, ~10-20%, especially >60y)
  - Herpes zoster ophthalmicus (keratitis, uveitis, secondary glaucoma, retinal necrosis)
  - Ramsay Hunt syndrome (geniculate ganglion: ear pain, vesicles in/on ear, facial palsy, hearing loss)
  - Bacterial superinfection (cellulitis, abscess, sepsis)
  - Disseminated zoster (visceral, pneumonitis, encephalitis) — rare, mostly immunocompromised
  - Motor neuropathy (localised weakness in affected limb, e.g.) — usually temporary
- **Chronic:**
  - Postherpetic neuralgia (pain >90 days) — refractory, disabling
  - Scarring / hypo- or hyperpigmentation
  - Chronic eye complications (scarring, visual loss)
sections.Prognosis: - Most immunocompetent patients recover with full resolution of rash and pain within 2–4 weeks. Mortality from shingles itself is <1% (mostly in disseminated disease). Postherpetic neuralgia risk increases steeply after age 60 — up to 20% of patients ≥70 years.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always ask about eye symptoms and look for Hutchinson's sign** — if positive, get slit-lamp that day.
- **Start antivirals as soon as possible, even if >72 hours, if new vesicles are still appearing or patient is immunocompromised.** Benefit extends beyond the 72-hour window in these groups.
- **Offer vaccination to eligible patients after acute episode resolves** — in the UK, the shingles vaccine (Shingrix) is recommended for those aged 65-70 and at-risk immunocompromised patients aged ≥50.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Prescribing oral steroids without concomitant antivirals** — may increase risk of dissemination.
- **Using adhesive dressings over vesicles** — can rupture them, delaying healing and increasing superinfection risk.
- **Assuming rash is only 'shingles' without checking for ocular involvement** in V1 distribution — missing this is a common cause of preventable visual loss.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - **Spread of rash to other dermatomes** or new vesicles appearing after day 5
  - **Fever, confusion, stiff neck, or breathing difficulty** — signs of dissemination/encephalitis
  - **Eye redness, pain, visual change** — immediate ophthalmology
  - **Worsening pain or new weakness** — possible motor involvement or postherpetic neuralgia
- **Follow-up:**
  - GP review in 1-2 weeks to check healing and pain control
  - If postherpetic neuralgia develops → refer to pain clinic if inadequate response to gabapentin/pregabalin
  - If ophthalmic zoster → follow-up with ophthalmology (usually 1 week, then as per their plan)
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / medical registrar if:**
  - **NEWS2 ≥5** (or individual component triggers)
  - **Lactate >2 mmol/L** (possible sepsis from superinfection)
  - **GCS drop ≤14** or new focal neurology (consider encephalitis)
  - **Suspected disseminated zoster** (widespread rash + systemic compromise) — may need ICU for management of organ failure
  - **Ophthalmic zoster with sight-threatening lesion** (e.g., dendritic ulcer, retinal necrosis) — urgent ophthalmology review (could be same-day)
- For rapid deterioration: activate local **Crash Team** per protocol (e.g., NEWS2 ≥7).

rawMarkdown:
## Shingles (Herpes Zoster)
**One-liner:** Reactivation of varicella-zoster virus causing a painful, unilateral vesicular rash in a single dermatome; common in elderly/immunocompromised, risk of postherpetic neuralgia.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Disseminated zoster** (visceral / pneumonitis / encephalitis) — key finding: **widespread vesicles beyond original dermatome** + systemic illness (fever, hypoxia, confusion)
- **Herpes zoster ophthalmicus** with acute retinal necrosis / keratitis — key finding: **vesicles on nose tip (Hutchinson's sign)** + eye pain, reduced vision, photophobia
- **Bacterial superinfection** leading to sepsis — key finding: **spreading cellulitic erythema, purulent exudate, fever** in an otherwise mild rash

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Prodrome: unilateral dermatomal pain / burning / paraesthesia (often precedes rash by 2-3 days)
  - Rash: crops of vesicles on erythematous base, **unilateral, in a single dermatome** (thoracic > trigeminal > lumbar)
  - Pain: constant / lancinating, may be severe
  - Systemic: malaise, low-grade fever, headache (mild unless disseminated)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Grouped vesicles (≤5 mm) that crust over 7-10 days, often leaving scarring / pigment change
  - Allodynia / hyperalgesia over the affected dermatome
  - Lymphadenopathy in draining nodes (occasional)
  - Signs of complications: eye redness, corneal ulcer, hearing loss (Ramsay Hunt), limb weakness (motor involvement)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - **Herpes simplex (HSV)** — recurrent, smaller clusters (often perioral / genital), **no dermatomal single-dermatome pattern**, typically milder pain before rash
  - **Contact dermatitis** — **itching > pain**, vesicular but not dermatomal, history of new topical agent / irritant
  - **Impetigo** — honey-crusted sores, **no dermatomal distribution**, often on face/limbs, more common in children
  - **Cellulitis** — diffuse erythema, swelling, warmth, **no vesicles**, no dermatomal border
  - **Varicella (chickenpox)** — widespread vesicles in different stages, **generalised pruritus**, systemic illness in children/adults
- **Splitter:** one feature that sharply differentiates top two (zoster vs HSV): **single dermatome involvement with prodromal pain** — zoster; recurrent episodes at same site + smaller clusters — HSV. If unsure, swab for viral PCR.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** Clinical diagnosis sufficient in typical cases. Slit-lamp exam if any suspicion of eye involvement (trigeminal branch V1 → urgent ophthalmology).
- **Bloods:** Not needed for routine diagnosis. Consider FBC, CRP, U&E, LFT if immunocompromised or suspected disseminated disease.
- **Imaging:** None routinely. CT/MRI brain if suspected encephalitis / CNS involvement (uncommon).
- **Special tests:**
  - **Vesicle swab for varicella-zoster virus (VZV) PCR** — gold standard if atypical, immunocompromised, or treatment failure. (Use dry swab, unroof vesicle, swab base. Send to virology.)
  - **Tzanck smear** (multinucleated giant cells) — rapid but less specific; PCR preferred.
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:** No formal scoring; diagnosis clinical — dermatomal vesicular rash with prodromal pain.
- **Never-miss test:** **Slit-lamp examination** when rash is in V1 distribution (forehead, nose tip) — Hutchinson’s sign predicts eye involvement. Omission may lead to blindness.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If ophthalmic zoster suspected → **urgent ophthalmology referral** (within 1-2 hours)
  - Start analgesia (paracetamol + NSAID + neuropathic agent if pain severe)
  - Initiate antivirals within 72 hours of rash onset (or if immunocompromised, regardless of timing)
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Only admit if: widespread disease, immunocompromised, ophthalmic with need for IV therapy, severe pain requiring IV analgesia, or suspected dissemination/encephalitis
  - **IV aciclovir** for disseminated zoster, ophthalmic with keratitis/retinal involvement, immunocompromised patients (e.g. transplant, HIV, chemotherapy)
  - Daily review of rash progression, eye symptoms, pain scores
  - Pain management: adjuvant gabapentin/pregabalin if neuropathic component
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Complete 7 days of oral antiviral (see Drugs & Doses)
  - Analgesia stepped as needed: paracetamol → NSAID → topical lidocaine → gabapentin/pregabalin (for postherpetic neuralgia)
  - Advise: keep rash clean and dry, avoid scratching, no vigorous exercise until crusted
  - **Vaccination:** Offer VZV vaccine (Shingrix or Zostavax) for prevention of recurrences in at-risk patients ≥50 years (per UK NHS schedule)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: IV 0.9% sodium chloride 1 L/8h if receiving IV aciclovir (to reduce risk of crystalluria)
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD if >18 and mobilising poorly (or according to local policy)
  - Diet: normal, ensure adequate hydration
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly if stable, 1-2 hourly if unstable or on IV therapy

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Oral aciclovir** 800 mg 5 times daily for 7 days (NICE/BNF) — start within 72 hours of rash
  - **IV aciclovir** 10–15 mg/kg every 8 hours (usually 500 mg–1 g Q8H) — for disseminated/immunocompromised/ophthalmic (check local policy for exact dose)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Valaciclovir** 1 g TDS for 7 days (oral, better bioavailability)
  - **Famciclovir** 500 mg TDS for 7 days (oral)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Aciclovir: caution in renal impairment (dose adjustment required)
  - Valaciclovir: same renal caution
  - Interaction: probenecid increases aciclovir levels; avoid concurrent nephrotoxins (e.g., gentamicin, NSAIDs in high doses)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - **Aciclovir (oral):** CrCl 10–25 mL/min → 800 mg TDS; CrCl <10 → 800 mg BID (BNF)
  - **IV aciclovir:** CrCl 25–50: 10–15 mg/kg Q12H; CrCl 10–25: 10–15 mg/kg Q24H; CrCl <10: 5–7.5 mg/kg Q24H (check local policy)
  - Valaciclovir/famciclovir also require dose reduction in moderate–severe renal impairment (refer to BNF)
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **IV aciclovir — give too fast or without adequate hydration → acute renal failure due to crystalluria.** Ensure infusion over ≥1 hour, pre- and post-hydration, and monitor U&E daily. Also: **prescribing for ophthalmic zoster without urgent ophthalmology review** = delayed sight loss.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Postherpetic neuralgia (most common, ~10-20%, especially >60y)
  - Herpes zoster ophthalmicus (keratitis, uveitis, secondary glaucoma, retinal necrosis)
  - Ramsay Hunt syndrome (geniculate ganglion: ear pain, vesicles in/on ear, facial palsy, hearing loss)
  - Bacterial superinfection (cellulitis, abscess, sepsis)
  - Disseminated zoster (visceral, pneumonitis, encephalitis) — rare, mostly immunocompromised
  - Motor neuropathy (localised weakness in affected limb, e.g.) — usually temporary
- **Chronic:**
  - Postherpetic neuralgia (pain >90 days) — refractory, disabling
  - Scarring / hypo- or hyperpigmentation
  - Chronic eye complications (scarring, visual loss)

### Prognosis
- Most immunocompetent patients recover with full resolution of rash and pain within 2–4 weeks. Mortality from shingles itself is <1% (mostly in disseminated disease). Postherpetic neuralgia risk increases steeply after age 60 — up to 20% of patients ≥70 years.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always ask about eye symptoms and look for Hutchinson's sign** — if positive, get slit-lamp that day.
- **Start antivirals as soon as possible, even if >72 hours, if new vesicles are still appearing or patient is immunocompromised.** Benefit extends beyond the 72-hour window in these groups.
- **Offer vaccination to eligible patients after acute episode resolves** — in the UK, the shingles vaccine (Shingrix) is recommended for those aged 65-70 and at-risk immunocompromised patients aged ≥50.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Prescribing oral steroids without concomitant antivirals** — may increase risk of dissemination.
- **Using adhesive dressings over vesicles** — can rupture them, delaying healing and increasing superinfection risk.
- **Assuming rash is only 'shingles' without checking for ocular involvement** in V1 distribution — missing this is a common cause of preventable visual loss.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - **Spread of rash to other dermatomes** or new vesicles appearing after day 5
  - **Fever, confusion, stiff neck, or breathing difficulty** — signs of dissemination/encephalitis
  - **Eye redness, pain, visual change** — immediate ophthalmology
  - **Worsening pain or new weakness** — possible motor involvement or postherpetic neuralgia
- **Follow-up:**
  - GP review in 1-2 weeks to check healing and pain control
  - If postherpetic neuralgia develops → refer to pain clinic if inadequate response to gabapentin/pregabalin
  - If ophthalmic zoster → follow-up with ophthalmology (usually 1 week, then as per their plan)

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / medical registrar if:**
  - **NEWS2 ≥5** (or individual component triggers)
  - **Lactate >2 mmol/L** (possible sepsis from superinfection)
  - **GCS drop ≤14** or new focal neurology (consider encephalitis)
  - **Suspected disseminated zoster** (widespread rash + systemic compromise) — may need ICU for management of organ failure
  - **Ophthalmic zoster with sight-threatening lesion** (e.g., dendritic ulcer, retinal necrosis) — urgent ophthalmology review (could be same-day)
- For rapid deterioration: activate local **Crash Team** per protocol (e.g., NEWS2 ≥7).
